data.title: Watch woman save dogs before escaping flames herself during fire in Philippines
data.description: A woman is drawing praise online for saving her two dogs before escaping herself from a burning building in the Philippines. Viral images show the woman tossing the animals to safety and then finally climbing down a fire ladder during the December 10, 2025, incident.\n

data.title: WHO responds to agency chief’s alleged pledge to ‘strengthen ties’ with Taiwan
data.description:
The World Health Organization said on Wednesday that its position on Taiwan remained unchanged after Japan’s finance minister claimed that the health body’s chief executive intended to strengthen ties with the island.
On December 6, Japanese Minister of Finance Satsuki Katayama made a social media post in Japanese stating that during a visit to Tokyo, the WHO director general had “explicitly [stated] his intention to strengthen ties” with Taiwan.
The post was made following a forum in Tokyo on universal health coverage organised by the Japanese government and the WHO. The event was attended by WHO Director General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus.
In one of the photos attached to her post, Katayama was shown shaking hands with Tedros, who has been accused by some – most notably US President Donald Trump – of being biased towards Beijing.
In response to a query from the South China Morning Post about Katayama’s post, a WHO media representative said that the organisation’s relations with Taiwan would continue to follow the “status quo”.
“Our position hasn’t changed. Our position is that we have existing relations with Taiwanese health partners,” the representative said.
He said a working relationship between the WHO and Taiwanese health experts and counterparts had been “going on for quite a few years now” under the organisation’s legal framework.
He added that technical collaboration with Taiwanese experts had reached “high levels” during the Covid-19 pandemic and had continued both on Covid and “other fronts”.
“We’re a health agency, and we work with health counterparts, and that will continue.”
Public transcripts released by the WHO of Tedros’ speeches at the forum in Tokyo did not mention Taiwan.
The Post contacted Katayama for comment via email but did not immediately receive a response.


Most members of the WHO are members of the United Nations, but some non-UN members have been allowed to join the health organisation, including as associate members. The People’s Republic of China is the sole legitimate representative of China in both the UN and the WHO, based on resolutions passed in the early 1970s.
Taiwan attended the World Health Assembly – the WHO’s top decision-making body – as an observer from 2009 to 2016.
It has been excluded from the assembly since 2017 due to objections from Beijing after the 2016 election of former Taiwanese leader Tsai Ing-wen, who refused to adhere to the one-China principle.
Beijing sees the island as part of China that must be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to arming Taiwan.
Taiwan received international praise for its handling of the Covid-19 pandemic and containment of the coronavirus. Michael Ryan, former executive director of the WHO’s health emergencies programme, was among those hailing the island’s efforts.
“We are observing and watching and bringing Taiwanese colleagues into the technical networks so they can share their experience and they can both contribute their knowledge but also seek new knowledge from outside,” Ryan said at a briefing held in Geneva in April 2020.
Taiwan has campaigned to be included in the assembly meetings, but the WHO member states have voted against its participation nine consecutive times.
In May, the representative offices of Britain, Australia, Canada, the Czech Republic, Japan, France, Germany and Lithuania in Taipei released a joint statement supporting Taiwan’s “meaningful engagement” with the WHO.
The statement said that Taiwan’s isolation from the World Health Assembly was “entirely unjustified” and undermined inclusive global public health cooperation and security.
Also in May, Beijing’s foreign ministry said that arrangements for Taiwan’s participation in global health affairs had been made under the one-China principle, including participation by medical and health experts in WHO technical meetings.
This includes “unimpeded and full-fledged information exchange mechanisms with the WHO and countries in the world” as well as the ability to report information to the WHO, according to the ministry.
“The so-called gap in global anti-epidemic efforts is nothing but a politically driven lie,” the ministry said.

data.title: Thailand says its military drones struck a bunker and casino along Cambodian border
data.description: Aerial footage released by the Thai military shows what was described as drones striking a casino complex and a Cambodian military bunker near the nations’ shared border.\nThai officials said the recent clashes have killed at least 15 people and forced more than 500,000 to evacuate from border areas, where fighting continues despite a recent truce brokered by the US and Asean.\n

data.title: Netflix K-drama Pro Bono: Jung Kyung-ho shines as fallen star judge in breezy legal drama
data.description:
Lead cast: Jung Kyung-ho, So Ju-yeon
Latest Nielsen rating: 6.18 per cent
Just a few months after playing a labour rights lawyer in Oh My Ghost Clients, Jung Kyung-ho is back on screens as another jurist taking on human interest cases in the breezy legal drama Pro Bono.
Here, Jung is Kang Da-wit, who starts off the show as a star judge with a massive social media following. He works at the Seoul Central District Court, which he likes to dub his castle.


Handsome, tough on corruption and generous to his staff, Da-wit is beloved by all. He skilfully feigns modesty while secretly being very ambitious – his dream is to be appointed to the Supreme Court.
However, his carefully laid plans experience a major roadblock following an unexpected meet-up with an old primary school classmate. He wakes up after a boozy night to find an apple box stuffed with cash in the boot of his car, with no recollection of how it got there.
Da-wit’s boss forces him to quietly resign to avoid a scandal and soon he is headhunted by former flame Oh Jung-in (Lee Yoo-young, appearing in her second legal drama of the year following Law and the City), a partner at her father’s large law firm Oh & Partners.
He accepts the job, expecting a plush partner gig and the corner office that comes with it, but is dumbstruck when he is guided to his new space – a cluttered pro bono office filled with well-meaning but hapless lawyers and a number of stray dogs.

The dogs are related to some of their current case work, which includes a dispute between the guardians of a golden retriever and its former abusive owners.
Still bewildered by the breathless turn of events that have sent him from a judge’s seat to a back office, Da-wit is not very focused on the case to begin with. But his ambition has not left him; recognising that Oh & Partners hopes to score PR points through its pro bono work, he strikes a deal with Jung-in.
Da-wit will throw himself into the work for a year, and if he manages to win over 70 per cent of his cases, Oh & Partners will help him secure his cherished Supreme Court nomination.
He sets to work, joining forces with idealistic human rights lawyer Park Gi-ppeum (So Ju-yeon, Dr. Romantic season 3). While Da-wit has won over his other colleagues in the pro bono department, he has his work cut out for him with Gi-ppeum because they had a run-in while he was still a judge.

At the Seoul Central District Court, Da-wit graciously came to Gi-ppeum’s aid when she was struggling with a printer, only to do a one-eighty when he realised she was not a staff member – whose votes he was actively courting.
Things quickly get complicated during their first case, when the woman claiming her dog was kidnapped hires a hotshot lawyer who Da-wit knows has a habit of fabricating evidence.
While Gi-ppeum’s idealism falters in the courtroom, Da-wit’s experience and connections succeed in keeping their case alive.
As a legal drama, Pro Bono is largely an enjoyable one, sticking to an episodic structure through the cases Da-wit needs to win and a larger frame of his fall from grace, comeback and we can safely assume his moral development.

Speaking of that moral trajectory, these opening episodes briefly let us glance at Da-wit’s rural upbringing. He may seem cocky in the present, but he had a difficult childhood and made it to the bench after passing the bar exam without a college degree.
Rather than violent crimes, the show is so far sticking to cases of social injustice, the kind that elicit tears and heartfelt victories. Look no further than Da-wit and Gi-ppeum’s first case, which goes right for the heartstrings with its focus on an abused pup.
Jung’s previous hit Oh My Ghost Clients shared a similar thematic structure, the only clear difference being that show’s supernatural premise. That said, the actor is equally entertaining here and Pro Bono offers slightly more in the way of legalese and complex jurisprudence, one of the pleasures of this genre.

Jung’s latest courtroom foray, which has already opened to strong ratings, proves that legal shows remain a top draw in the world of K-dramas.
Pro Bono is streaming on Netflix.

data.title: Hong Kong vocal coach found guilty of molesting 7 male students during lessons
data.description:
A Hong Kong vocal instructor has been found guilty of molesting seven male students over a six-year period.
The District Court on Thursday convicted Drawson Lam Chun-yat of nine counts of indecent assault and two of common assault for the offences committed in his three classrooms in Cheung Sha Wan and Kwai Chung between 2018 and 2023.
Lam, 39, was known as a contestant in the debut season of Midlife, Sing & Shine, a televised singing competition organised by TVB, which aired in 2022. He was eliminated in the preliminary round.
The instructor was arrested upon returning from mainland China in July 2023, after a 15-year-old boy from Taiwan filed a police report about being groped by him on two occasions earlier that year.
The case garnered widespread media attention and prompted 10 other complainants to come forward. One of them, a 34-year-old man, said Lam assaulted him when he was a student interviewing the instructor as part of a school assignment in 2008.
Deputy Judge Bernard Chung Wai-keung found the allegations by seven of the 11 complainants were substantiated beyond a reasonable doubt, including those made by the boy, who is now 18.
During the trial, the court heard how Lam forcefully removed the teenager’s pants and touched his genitals under the pretence of conducting a medical inspection in May 2023.
When the defendant assaulted the boy again two months later, he reportedly justified the physical contact as a necessary step towards stardom.

“You’re being groped like this because you’re not famous,” the boy quoted Lam as saying. “You’ll only become popular after being groped by others. This is very normal.”
Chung accepted the boy’s evidence and ruled the inspection of male genitalia could not be part of regular voice training.
Lam was also found guilty of molesting a 30-year-old clerk by purporting to massage his genitals in front of his then-girlfriend in 2020.
The court found insufficient evidence to support eight other counts of indecent assault and one of common assault arising from the complaints of the remaining four witnesses.
Chung said he had doubts about the 34-year-old man’s allegation, noting that he had continued to meet the defendant on several occasions after the alleged assault in 2008.
The deputy judge added that he could not be sure whether the defendant genuinely believed the complainant had consented to the contact, as the witness had neither cried for help nor attempted to escape.
The court ordered an impact assessment for the seven victims, along with reports on Lam’s background and psychological condition, before sentencing on January 26.
Lam had been remanded in custody for 26 months before being granted bail in September this year.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest man over trying to open plane door on Cathay flight
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a man for allegedly trying to open a plane cabin door during a Cathay Pacific Airways flight from Boston.
The force said it received a report from the city’s flagship carrier at around 4.45am on Thursday about the incident.
The plane landed at Hong Kong airport at around 5am, with police officers boarding the aircraft to arrest the suspect, a 20-year-old mainland Chinese man, for allegedly violating the Aviation Security Ordinance.
The case has been classified as a “request for police assistance” and is being handled by the force’s airport district investigation team.
In a written reply to the Post, Cathay Pacific said that a passenger was suspected of attempting to open an aircraft door on flight CX811, which departed from Boston on Wednesday.
The cabin crew immediately attended to the situation, inspected the door to ensure it was securely closed, and reported the incident to the relevant authorities and police, it said.
“No crew members or passengers were injured, and the flight landed safely at Hong Kong International Airport [in the] early morning of Thursday,” it said.
According to the Aviation Security Ordinance, it is an offence to wilfully tamper or interfere with any aircraft component, apparatus, equipment or on-board systems whilst on a plane.
The maximum penalty is two years in prison and a HK$50,000 (US$6,425) fine.

data.title: Hong Kong set to keep its IPO crown in 2026 on mega deals and return of US capital: UBS
data.description:
Hong Kong’s initial public offering (IPO) market is on track to maintain its status as the world’s top fundraising venue next year, underpinned by a pipeline of mega deals and the return of US capital, according to UBS.
The Swiss investment bank expects more than HK$300 billion (US$38.5 billion) to be raised from 150 to 200 IPOs in 2026, surpassing the roughly HK$270 billion raised this year, which vaulted the city back to No 1 globally.
UBS also forecast that the city’s benchmark Hang Seng Index could climb to 30,000 points next year, implying a 17.5 per cent gain from Thursday’s close of 25,530.
“The first quarter of next year will see some IPOs raise over HK$10 billion,” said John Lee Chen-kwok, co-head of Asia country coverage at UBS Global Banking. Deals in consumer goods, high technology and artificial intelligence should give the market a strong start, he added.
Asked whether any float could top Contemporary Amperex Technology’s HK$41 billion Hong Kong share sale – the world’s largest this year – Lee said such an outcome could “not be excluded”.
While KPMG has issued a more bullish forecast of up to HK$350 billion in proceeds, Lee said UBS still expected Hong Kong to keep its crown among global IPO markets next year. One key driver, he noted, was room for global investors to rebuild positions in Greater China after modest inflows earlier in 2025 left them still underweight.
US funds had already begun to return in the second half of this year amid a renewed rally over Chinese technology stocks, according to Lee.
Despite ongoing trade friction, American investors remained keen on China-related opportunities and were likely to increase allocations, he said.

However, he cautioned that flows were unlikely to revert quickly to the exuberant levels of 2021.
UBS also expected the restart of the US Federal Reserve’s interest rate-cutting cycle and hoped for a gradual recovery in China’s economy to sustain momentum in Hong Kong stocks and primary deals.
On Wednesday, the World Bank raised its 2025 forecast for China’s gross domestic product growth to 4.9 per cent, citing recent fiscal stimulus and signs of stabilising global trade policies.
Concerns about listing quality resurfaced this week after reports that Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) and the Securities and Futures Commission had urged IPO sponsors to pay close attention to disclosure standards, stoking questions about the calibre of applicants to the city’s market.
HKEX said on Wednesday it was committed to ensuring that information submitted by issuers was complete and of high quality.
Lee said the regulators’ letter was intended as a reminder to intermediaries not to cut corners amid heavy workloads, rather than a signal of systemic problems. He added that he remained confident about the overall quality of IPO applicants, given the stringent vetting processes in both Hong Kong and mainland China.

data.title: Adrian Cheng signs retail partnership with Wafi Group, headed by Dubai royal
data.description:
Adrian Cheng Chi-kong, former heir apparent to Hong Kong property giant New World Development (NWD), has partnered with a Dubai royal and businessman to spruce up the retail, leisure and cultural offerings of Wafi City, one of the United Arab Emirates (UAE) city’s top shopping destinations.
Cheng and Dubai Sheikh Mana bin Khalifa Al Maktoum, founder and chairman of the Wafi Group, signed an agreement to set up a new entity called Wafi Anime 11 in Dubai on Wednesday, according to a statement on Thursday.
Al Maktoum is the cousin of Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, who also serves as the vice-president and prime minister of the UAE.
Under the multi-project partnership, Cheng-founded investment firm Almad Group would “bring its globally renowned approach to cultural retail and entertainment to a new region, with Wafi City and Dubai acting as the nucleus of the expansion”, the statement said.
“The two conglomerates will unite to usher in a new era for Wafi City, where heritage, culture and innovation will converge, strengthening Wafi Group’s position as the creator of one of Dubai’s most distinctive retail, leisure and wellness destinations.”
The joint venture planned to draw on Cheng’s K11 by AC brand, which is showcased in the K11 Musea mall in Hong Kong’s Tsim Sha Tsui.
“We are honoured to partner with Wafi Group, the pioneer of retail and iconic mixed-use development in Dubai,” Cheng said. “This collaboration at Wafi City sets a new benchmark for cross-regional retail cooperation, as we connect the consumers and rich cultural industries of the Middle East and Chinese-speaking markets.”
Wafi Anime 11 would seek to capitalise on growing interest in anime-themed retail and entertainment in the global market and the UAE, the partners said.
The agreement would also cover a leasing partnership to help Chinese brands enter the UAE and the region, as well as a tourism strategy to offer Chinese travellers perks and incentives to explore Wafi City and stay in its hotels, which include Sofitel Dubai The Obelisk and Raffles Dubai, they added.
Launched in 1991, Wafi City has a gross leasable area of 1.1 million sq ft and a gross floor area of 1.6 million sq ft. It recorded a 30 per cent increase in footfall over the past three years.
Almad said it aimed to introduce a range of Chinese brands to the shopping centre, including ZBR Bronze, Master Zhu Bingren and ChaPanda.
The joint venture also covers the establishment at Raffles Dubai of the Gentry Club, “a high-privacy exclusive club” serving high-net-worth individuals and culture lovers, the partners said. It will be the club’s first private-member experience outside Greater China.
“Wafi Group has always embodied heritage, innovation and the spirit of Dubai,” Al Maktoum said. “The joint collaborations with Almad Group and K11 by AC reflect our continued commitment to shaping the future of retail and lifestyle in the region.”
Cheng announced the establishment of Almad in September, nearly three months after the third-generation scion of NWD resigned as non-executive director of the company. Almad would target markets in Southeast Asia, the Middle East and China with a mission to break “boundaries in digital and virtual assets”, the company said last month. Almad operates a digital-asset arm, A2Z.
One month after the formation of Almad, he became a shareholder in CBCX Group, a London-headquartered multi-asset broker. Founded in 2011, CBCX offers a trading platform for currencies, precious metals, stocks, indices and commodities. It is also expanding into the digital-asset ecosystem.
Cheng stepped down as CEO of NWD a year ago after the company reported a record full-year loss of about US$2.5 billion amid a property slump in Hong Kong and mainland China.

data.title: Proposals on territorial concessions by Ukraine sent to Trump, Germany’s Merz says
data.description:
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Thursday that US President Donald Trump had been sent a proposal on territorial concessions Ukraine is ready to make to end its war against Russia.
Merz said the proposal was sent after he and other European leaders had spoken by phone with Trump on Wednesday.
“It mainly concerns the question of what territorial concessions Ukraine is prepared to make,” Merz said. He cautioned however that ultimately “the Ukrainian president and the Ukrainian people have to answer the question”.
Merz, in a joint press conference with Nato chief Mark Rutte, said “it would be a mistake to force the Ukrainian president into a peace that his people will not accept after four years of suffering and death”.
On Wednesday, Trump expressed impatience with Ukraine and its European allies France, Britain and Germany.
Trump said “strong words” were exchanged in the phone call with Merz, British Prime Minister Keir Starmer and French President Emmanuel Macron.

Merz said that further talks with the Americans were planned this weekend and that an international meeting on Ukraine “could take place at the beginning of next week”.
“Whether the American government participates or not very much depends on the joint drafts of papers which are currently being worked on,” he said.
Merz said his conversation with Trump on Wednesday had “left the strong impression that he is ready to go down this path with us, because he knows that the Europeans and their interests have to be heard”.

data.title: In Chloe Zhao’s Hamnet, Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal went on an emotional ‘epic journey’
data.description:
During the emotionally wrecking final scene of Hamnet, Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal had an issue.
“There were moments where the camera was obstructing us,” Buckley recalls. “We were like: ‘No, we have to see each other.’
“And then the minute we did see each other, it was like ‘Oh, no,’” Mescal says, laughing. “What a glorious thing.”
In Hamnet, Chloe Zhao’s adaptation of Maggie O’Farrell’s prize-winning 2020 novel, Mescal plays William Shakespeare and Buckley his wife, Agnes. It is a fictional, speculative drama with a basis in historical fact.


One of the couple’s three children, Hamnet, died in 1596 at the age of 11. Within a handful of years, Hamlet would premiere at the Globe Theatre in London. The names, scholars have noted, were essentially interchangeable in 16th-century England.
Zhao’s film imagines the possible connection between the death of Shakespeare’s son and the birth of one of the playwright’s greatest works. It is a portrait of a marriage, in grief and literary greatness. In many ways, it is also a movie about seeing and being seen.
Both William and Agnes are drawn together as misunderstood near-outcasts. William is dismissed as “a pasty-faced scholar”. Agnes is branded a “forest witch”. Gaps of misperception and solitude are bridged by love in the film’s first half, and art in its overwhelming final act.
In both cases, Buckley’s and Mescal’s eyes tell much of the story. Their performances – raw, earthy, soulful – have been hailed as among the best of the year. Both are widely expected to land Oscar nominations. Though they acted in separate timelines in Maggie Gyllenhaal’s The Lost Daughter (2021), Hamnet is the first time on screen together for the two young, acclaimed Irish stars.
“We entered the film at the perfect juncture. I had huge respect for Jessie and loved spending time with her,” Mescal says. “But we were also at the point where we didn’t know each other all that well. So there was a kind of mystery.”

Before beginning production, Zhao staged a chemistry read for the two of them. It might go down as the most unnecessary chemistry read in Hollywood history.
“We’d forget that we were saying lines,” says Mescal, sitting beside Buckley.
“There was such a kinetic energy already between us,” Buckley agrees. “It just felt so possible.”
Buckley and Mescal met this reporter earlier this autumn, just as Hamnet was making its prize-winning premiere at the Toronto International Film Festival.
The film has since garnered six Golden Globe award nominations, including best performance by a female actor in a motion picture (drama); and best performance by a male actor in a supporting role in a motion picture (drama).
But Buckley, the 35-year-old star of Wild Rose and Wicked Little Letters, and the 29-year-old Mescal, who was soon to embark on playing Paul McCartney in Sam Mendes’ four-film series, entered jovial and wisecracking. Buckley, a new mother, remarked on nursing before requesting it not be printed, and then reversing herself. “Ah, print it. What do I care!”

Once they settled in, though, both actors struggled to capture the enormity of their experience making Hamnet. If the film has moved audiences, it has shaken its stars.
“We worked with Kim [Gillingham, a coach] for subconscious and dreams. She asks you these prompts as you start to work. One was, ‘Why are you making it?’” Mescal recalls, turning towards Buckley.
“I don’t want to get into why I initially thought I was making it. But I remember sitting with you and looking up at the stars two weeks in. Something personal had been going on in my life before. I remember turning to you and going, ‘Oh, that thought was way too small.’”
Little is known about Shakespeare’s life, and even less about Agnes’s. That meant the actors were drawing on their own experiences as artists to try to better understand their characters. Each day of production, Zhao led the cast in a meditation of three deep breaths, a practice she has continued at screenings.
“As actors, sometimes people just want you to wear a mask and put coats on, and I never find that satisfying,” Buckley says. “What Chloe wants you to do is move somewhere deeper within yourself to meet the person you’re going to come to understand.
“It’s not about masks. If anything, it’s about becoming more human and pulling off a layer of skin that you’ve maybe kept around yourself too tightly.”

Zhao, the Oscar-winning Nomadland director who directed the Marvel movie Eternals, says she challenged Mescal and Buckley to play “extreme masculine and extreme feminine”. Since emerging with a pair of Native American dramas, Songs My Brothers Taught Me and The Rider, Zhao has refined a rough-hewn naturalism that actors gravitate towards. To find a very different Shakespeare, she relied on those instincts.
“What we have to do as artists is try to find that commonality that transcends time and space and gender and religion,” Zhao says. “You say: what is the bone-deep humanity of that man that is also in Paul Mescal? That’s my job, to open that portal.”
Hamnet dares to suppose that all art, even something as epochal as Hamlet, comes from somewhere profoundly personal. Mescal’s Shakespeare, for instance, does not go around eloquently spouting verses.
“How boring would that be!” Mescal says. “Anyone who writes like that isn’t walking around waxing lyrical. I think there’s a real engine underneath him. There’s someone who wanted to escape his life and love his life at the same time. He loved his life and loved his work. This constant conflict in him, charging around his life, restless-like.”
“That’s also you,” Buckley interjects.
“That was just the version of him that made the most sense to me,” Mescal responds. “I’m sure there will be someone at Oxford who’ll be like, ‘He would have spoken with a weird hybrid accent because of the time period.’ OK, whatever. I don’t care.”

Hamnet reaches a remarkable crescendo in a performance of Hamlet at the Globe, which opens up deep wells of sorrow and oceans of empathy.
It has already become one of the most talked-about finales of the year, and it is when the movie’s belief in the transference of feeling becomes most palpable. Yet, cameras aside, it was a scene they struggled to find.
“To be completely honest, we had gone on this ginormous, epic journey of the heart. We got to the Globe. I had not a clue what to do. I was totally lost. I think Chloe was lost,” Buckley says. “The Globe flooded. There was a rainstorm for two days. You go on this huge journey, and you’re like: where does this end?”
But on the third day, something clicked for Buckley when composer Max Richter’s “On the Nature of Daylight” came on her playlist. She shared it with Zhao, and something shifted.
“Sometimes as an actor, you feel like you have to do it yourself,” Buckley says. “She was like this lone wolf in the middle of this ocean of people. I realised on the third day how everyone around me was crucial. It became about surrendering to the community of feeling.”
Buckley and Mescal have already resolved to work together again.
“I feel like we’re going to meet each other at pinnacles of our lives and help each other unravel the next layer,” Buckley says.
“Hands down, it was one of the most important collaborations that I’ve ever had,” Mescal says. “It’d be insane for that to be the only time we did it.”
But it is also possible that the final moments of Hamnet will forever stay with them. The scene’s power is owed, also, to the hundreds of extras who play a poignant role. Buckley’s and Mescal’s eyes are locked on one another, but the play is the thing – not just its transmutation of their private grief, but the play’s resonance with all around them.
“Why we go to the cinema, why we go to the theatre, why we tell stories is for these places to contain the parts of ourselves that are too hard to hold by ourselves,” Buckley says. “There’s this unspoken ocean between the person sitting next to you and the story, and the play is the vessel through which that transcends.”
She shakes her head. “It was incredible. Standing at the lip of the stage, I could just feel a tsunami of 300 people behind me opening up their hearts.”
